q: A particle of mass m moves in a horizontal circle of radius r with speed v. If radius is doubled keeping speed constant, centripetal force becomes:
exp: F = mv²/r, so doubling r halves the force.

q: A stone tied to a string is whirled in a vertical circle. At the highest point, minimum speed required to just complete the circle is:
exp: At top, tension zero ⇒ mv²/r = mg ⇒ v = √(gr).

q: In uniform circular motion, which quantity remains constant?
exp: Speed remains constant, direction changes continuously.

q: The direction of centripetal acceleration is always:
exp: Centripetal acceleration always points toward the center.

q: A car takes a circular turn on a flat road. Maximum speed without skidding depends on:
exp: v_max = √(μgr).

q: A particle completes one revolution in time T. Its angular speed is:
exp: ω = 2π/T.

q: A body in circular motion experiences work done by centripetal force equal to:
exp: Force is perpendicular to displacement at every instant.

q: A particle moves in a circle with constant speed v. Its angular momentum about center is proportional to:
exp: L = mvr ⇒ proportional to r.

q: A cyclist bends while taking a turn to:
exp: Bending helps align resultant force through center of mass.

q: A particle moves in a circle of radius r with angular velocity ω. Linear speed is:
exp: v = rω.

q: If speed of a particle in circular motion doubles, centripetal force becomes:
exp: F ∝ v².

q: A conical pendulum has string length L and angle θ. Its angular speed is:
exp: From force balance, ω² = g tanθ / L.

q: The centripetal force in a vertical circle is minimum at:
exp: At top, only mg contributes toward center.

q: A body moves in a circle with decreasing speed. Acceleration has:
exp: Changing speed introduces tangential acceleration.

q: Time period of a particle in uniform circular motion is independent of:
exp: T = 2π/ω, independent of mass.

q: A satellite orbiting Earth has centripetal force provided by:
exp: Gravity acts as centripetal force in orbit.

q: A particle in circular motion has zero:
exp: After complete revolution, net displacement is zero.

q: If radius of circular motion is halved and speed unchanged, angular velocity becomes:
exp: ω = v/r, so inversely proportional to r.

q: A particle moves in a circle. The direction of velocity is:
exp: Velocity is always tangent to path.

q: A car of mass m takes a circular turn of radius r on a banked road without friction at speed v. Condition is:
exp: Banking condition gives tanθ = v²/gr.

q: Angular momentum of a particle in circular motion remains constant when:
exp: Conservation of angular momentum requires zero external torque.

q: A particle completes 10 revolutions per second. Its angular velocity is:
exp: ω = 2πf = 20π rad/s.

q: A stone tied to a string breaks at bottom of vertical circle. Tension is maximum at:
exp: At bottom, tension = mg + mv²/r (maximum).

q: Centripetal force is directed:
exp: It always points toward center of circular path.

q: A particle moves in circle with constant speed. Which is true?
exp: Force is perpendicular to motion, so work done is zero.

q: If mass of rotating particle is doubled, centripetal force becomes:
exp: F ∝ m.

q: A particle moving in a circle experiences torque about center:
exp: Force passes through center, so torque is zero.

q: A body moves in circular path of radius r. If r increases, angular velocity for same speed:
exp: ω = v/r, so decreases.

q: A satellite in lower orbit moves faster because:
exp: Stronger gravitational force at lower orbit requires higher speed.

q: A particle in circular motion has velocity and acceleration vectors at right angle. This implies:
exp: Perpendicular acceleration means only direction changes.

q: A body completes circular motion in 2 s. Frequency is:
A particle in circular motion has kinetic energy proportional to:
exp: KE depends on speed, not radius directly.

q: A roller coaster at top of loop just maintains contact when:
exp: Minimum speed condition at top of loop.

q: Angular displacement is measured in:
exp: SI unit of angular displacement is radian.

q: If centripetal force suddenly vanishes, particle will:
exp: Without centripetal force, object moves along tangent.

q: A particle in circular motion has constant:
exp: Only magnitude of velocity remains constant.

q: A particle moves in circle of radius 2 m with speed 4 m/s. Angular acceleration is:
exp: Uniform circular motion has zero angular acceleration.

q: A particle completes half circle. Displacement magnitude is:
exp: Straight-line distance between opposite points is diameter = 2r.

q: Centripetal acceleration is proportional to:
exp: a = v²/r.

q: A particle moving in circle has momentum direction:
exp: Momentum is along velocity direction.

q: A body rotates with constant angular velocity. Its angular acceleration is:
exp: Constant ω implies zero angular acceleration.

q: A car moving on flat circular road depends on friction because:
exp: Friction supplies necessary centripetal force.

q: A particle in circular motion has acceleration directed:
exp: Centripetal acceleration is inward.

q: If angular velocity doubles, centripetal acceleration becomes:
exp: a = ω²r.

q: A body moving in circle has constant:
exp: Speed remains constant in uniform circular motion.

q: A particle completes 1 revolution. Work done by centripetal force is:
exp: Force is perpendicular to displacement throughout.

q: A particle moving in circle has maximum linear speed at:
exp: Uniform circular motion has constant speed.

q: A body in circular motion is an example of:
exp: Continuous change in direction implies acceleration.

q: A satellite orbiting Earth is kept in orbit due to:
exp: Gravity provides required centripetal force.

q: A particle in circular motion has angular momentum:
exp: In absence of external torque, angular momentum is conserved.

q: A particle moving in circle suddenly doubles its radius keeping angular momentum constant. Speed becomes:
exp: L = mvr ⇒ v ∝ 1/r.
